plant_parts: Plant Parts is the most common concept in the lesson library, appearing in 239 lessons that teach plant anatomy — roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits, and seeds — across grades K–5. It sits squarely in science with no overlap or naming problems. Roots and Root Vegetables are related but more specific concepts that lessons use deliberately, so they are not duplicates of this one. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
cultural_traditions: Cultural Traditions is the second most-used concept in the library, tagging lessons that engage with a cultural practice, holiday, or food heritage — from Lunar New Year and Black History Month to Three Sisters, hummus, Aloo Gobi, and Thanksgiving lessons. Two concepts that each appear in only one lesson — Family Traditions (a tamales lesson) and International Food Traditions (a fattoush lesson) — are just narrower versions of this same idea, and both lessons already carry the Cultural Traditions tag, so those two can safely fold into this one. Cultural Narratives (8 lessons) is deliberately kept separate: nearly all of its lessons pair it with Storytelling and center on reading, writing, or telling a culturally rooted story — a literacy angle, distinct from this broader social-studies tag. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
nutrition_education: Nutrition Education covers lessons about nutrition and healthy eating and appears in over 100 lessons, almost all under the Health subject. It is currently tagged 100 times under Health and 7 times under Science, but the Science group isn't a real biology-of-nutrition collection: 6 of those 7 are cultural recipe lessons (Agua Fresca, All About Corn, Juneteenth, ice cream history, Carbon Footprint Tacos, Plants as Medicine) where the tag landed under Science as a labeling side effect, and the seventh (a glucose-regulation lesson) is already tagged under Health too. So treating this as a Health-only concept loses nothing. If a future lesson genuinely teaches the biology of nutrition — digestion, metabolism, macronutrients — a new, more specific science concept would fit better than stretching this one. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
visual_arts: Visual Arts tags lessons where students create something visual — drawing, painting, collage, garden journaling, or plant illustration. Two concepts that each appear in only one lesson make sense to fold in here: Coloring (a coloring activity on a pre-K lesson that already carries the Drawing tag, so nothing is lost) and Craft Activity (a paper-cutout winter garden bed — too vague to stand on its own). Drawing itself (9 lessons) is deliberately kept separate as a distinct visual-arts technique. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
storytelling: Storytelling appears in about 75 lessons and covers narrative and oral-sharing activities — picture-book read-alouds (The Carrot Seed, Spring After Spring), sharing personal food memories, letter writing, and reading cultural stories. It is almost entirely a literacy concept: only one lesson out of 75 also carries it under Arts, and that lesson is so thinly described (a one-sentence summary with empty body text) that there is no real evidence of a theater- or performance-style storytelling angle anywhere in the library. So this should live under Literacy/English Language Arts only; if the curriculum ever needs a genuinely theatrical storytelling concept, something like Puppetry or a new dramatic-performance concept would be the right home for it. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
ecosystems: Ecosystems covers lessons about how living things depend on each other — food webs, habitats, biodiversity, and thinking in ecological systems. Food Webs (11 lessons) and Biodiversity (tagged 7 times) are deliberately separate concepts with narrower focuses — who-eats-whom relationships and variety of species, respectively — not duplicates of this one. One wrinkle: "Ecosystems" is also the name of one of the library's theme categories, and whether the concept and the theme should be reconciled is a separate decision that belongs with the review of the themes. For now: keep as its own concept.
recipe_reading: Recipe Reading tags lessons where students read a recipe as step-by-step text — following ordered instructions and decoding ingredient lists while they cook — confirmed across sample lessons spanning grades K–5. Three related concepts are deliberately kept separate because they are genuinely different skills, not other names for this one: Recipe Writing (2 lessons), where students compose their own recipes from personal or cultural knowledge; Recipe Selection (1 lesson), where students vote among recipes they designed; and Recipe Scaling (3 lessons), which is a math concept about fractions and ratios for adjusting quantities, not part of this reading-and-writing family at all. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
historical_figures: Historical Figures appears in about 69 lessons and covers people who shaped history — Cesar Chavez, George Washington Carver, Black History Month figures, LGBTQ activists, and the staple-crop growers of ancient civilizations. Nearly all of those lessons (68) sit under Social Studies, which fits the heart of the concept: people as makers of cultural, civic, or scientific change. A single lesson ("In the Garden with Dr. Carver") was tagged under Literacy instead, but it teaches the same person through a picture-book read-aloud, and that read-aloud angle is already covered by Storytelling — if reading biographies were truly its own literacy skill here, we would expect dozens of such lessons, not one. So this should live under Social Studies only. A related one-lesson concept, Historical Context, might eventually fold into this one; that question is left for the review of the rarely used concepts. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
measurement: Measurement is the most common math concept in the library, appearing in 66 lessons — almost half of all math tagging — and covers measuring length, volume, weight, temperature, time, area, and perimeter, usually while cooking or gardening. The closest related math concepts — Weight (1 lesson), Volume (2 lessons), Area (1 lesson), Perimeter (1 lesson) — are more specific tags that lessons use deliberately, not duplicates of this general one. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
vocabulary_development: Vocabulary Development tags lessons built around word learning — naming, defining, and expanding students' vocabulary in cooking and garden contexts. It belongs purely to literacy, with no overlap or naming problems. Related literacy concepts — Reading Comprehension (6 lessons), Descriptive Language (1 lesson), Figurative Language (1 lesson), Sensory Details (1 lesson) — are recognizably different literacy practices and stay separate. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
geography: Geography tags lessons that explore regional, national, and global geography — usually framed around where a food or ingredient comes from. It sits cleanly under Social Studies with no overlap or naming problems. Map Reading (1 lesson) and Trade Routes (16 lessons) are deliberately narrower related concepts kept separate. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
decomposition: Decomposition covers composting, the breakdown of organic matter, microbe activity, and nutrient cycling in soil — typically tied to school-garden compost programs. It sits cleanly in science with no overlap or naming problems. Microorganisms (3 lessons) is a deliberately narrower related concept about microbe biology and stays separate. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
soil_science: Soil Science covers soil composition, soil health, soil layers, soil organisms, and how soil and plants interact. The one-lesson concept Soil may turn out to be just another name for this one, but a single lesson is too little evidence to call it now — that question is left for the review of the rarely used concepts. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
life_cycles: Life Cycles covers how living things grow and change over time, and appears in 43 lessons. It spans plant life cycles (seed saving, seed starting, seasonal growing), animal life cycles (butterfly metamorphosis, bees), and lessons that combine plants and animals. Two other concepts turn out to be the same idea under different wording: **Growing Cycles** (tagged once, on a lesson about the origins of seasonal plants) and **Growth Patterns** (tagged once, on a lesson about how plants grow in different directions) are both really about plant development over time, which Life Cycles already covers. We recommend keeping Life Cycles as its own concept, with Growing Cycles and Growth Patterns folding into it.
plant_needs: Plant Needs covers what plants require to live and grow — sun, water, soil, air, and nutrients — and is one of the most frequently used science concepts. It stands cleanly on its own, with nothing duplicating it. The related concept **Plant Nutrition** (tagged twice) is clearly narrower — it focuses specifically on how plants take up nutrients — and stays separate. We recommend keeping Plant Needs as its own concept.
immigration_stories: Immigration Stories covers lessons built around immigrant food, family, and cultural stories, and is one of the most frequently used social studies concepts. It typically appears alongside **Cultural Traditions** — for example, the Food Roots lesson, Hummus and Pita, and lessons about the history of food workers. It has a clear home of its own with nothing else overlapping or duplicating it, so we recommend keeping it as its own concept.
healthy_choices: Healthy Choices covers lessons where students practice making decisions about food and lifestyle — snack choices, water versus sugary drinks, balanced plates, and looking critically at food marketing — and is one of the most frequently used health concepts. The related concept **Hydration** (tagged once) is clearly narrower, focused specifically on drinking enough water, and stays separate. We recommend keeping Healthy Choices as its own concept.
pollinators: Pollinators covers bees, butterflies, hummingbirds, and how pollination works, usually in bee-habitat and pollinator-garden lessons. It stands cleanly on its own, with nothing duplicating it. It is distinct from **Beneficial Insect Identification** (tagged once), which takes a more specific identifying-insects angle, and from **Biodiversity** (tagged 7 times across science and social studies lessons). We recommend keeping Pollinators as its own concept.
counting: Counting covers early-grade counting and number-recognition activities — counting seeds, vegetables, plates, or recipe ingredients, mostly in pre-K through 2nd grade lessons. The related concepts **Tallying** (tagged once) and **Categorization** (tagged once) are genuinely different early-math practices, so they stay separate. We recommend keeping Counting as its own concept.
data_collection: Data Collection covers lessons where students gather and record information — plant-growth measurements, taste-test responses, or class poll results. The related concepts **Graphing** (tagged 3 times) and **Sorting** (tagged once) are the next steps students take after collecting data, and they stay separate. We recommend keeping Data Collection as its own concept.
water_cycles: Water Cycles covers the water cycle — evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and runoff — usually in lessons about garden watering and weather patterns. It stands cleanly on its own with nothing overlapping it, so we recommend keeping it as its own concept. One small naming question: the tag is plural ("Water Cycles") because that matches the wording the lessons themselves use most, but since there is really one water cycle, the team may prefer the singular "Water Cycle." Either way works — it's a low-stakes wording call.
trade_routes: Trade Routes covers historical trade and where foods came from — mapping the Silk Road, the Columbian Exchange, and foods crossing the Atlantic. It is closely related to **Geography** (which appears in 56 lessons) but clearly narrower, focusing specifically on the history of trade routes. We recommend keeping Trade Routes as its own concept.
estimation: Estimation covers lessons where students estimate quantities (seeds in a packet, vegetables in a garden bed), times (how long something cooks), or measurements (about how many cups) while cooking and gardening. It stands cleanly on its own, with nothing duplicating it. It pairs naturally with **Measurement** (which appears in 66 lessons) — students often estimate first, then measure precisely — but the two are distinct. We recommend keeping Estimation as its own concept.
community_systems:
Community Systems covers lessons that look at how communities, food, and the environment work together as systems — from farm and food workers to community gardens, food justice, and what it means to be part of a community. It is currently a broad bucket holding several related angles: farm and food workers (Cesar Chavez, Booker T. Whatley's community-supported farm model, School Food Heroes), food and environmental systems (the food system's environmental impact, watersheds, biodiversity, the Lorax debate), community gardens and food justice, and community membership norms. The common thread is systems thinking applied to community, food, and the environment.

Two tiny concepts fold in cleanly: **Community** (tagged once, on a mural-painting lesson about shared public space) and **Community Building** (tagged once, on a classroom-welcome lesson) — neither appears often enough to stand alone. A third, **Community Activism** (tagged once), should instead fold into **Advocacy** (tagged 3 times), because it means essentially the same thing as advocacy rather than being a piece of community systems. We recommend keeping Community Systems as its own concept. Down the road, the team may want to sharpen the line between Community Systems, **Food Systems**, and **Environmental Justice**, since lessons currently use those three in overlapping ways.
seeds: Seeds is a broad science concept covering seed anatomy (dissecting a lima bean in "Seed Maps"), what seeds need to grow, edible seeds (lessons like "Cooking with Seeds: Ful Medames" and "Lentil Wraps"), seed sorting, seed saving and the origins of agriculture, and planting seeds in the garden. Two neighboring concepts deliberately stay separate rather than folding in: **Seed Dispersal** (tagged 3 times) looks at how seeds travel — by water, wind, or hitching rides on animals, all taught through model-building activities — and **Seed Starting** (tagged once) is about sprouting seeds for transplanting. All three are different scientific angles on seeds, so each keeps its own entry. We recommend keeping Seeds as its own concept.
companion_planting: Companion Planting covers planting crops that help each other grow — most famously the Three Sisters trio of corn, beans, and squash — and appears in 13 lessons. Eleven of the 13 center on Three Sisters, Lenape, and Squanto Indigenous-agriculture teaching, plus one general garden-patterns lesson and one cooking variant. These lessons are currently split between science and social studies, but the split came from inconsistent tagging rather than a real difference — nearly identical lessons landed on opposite subjects ("3 Sisters Tacos" under social studies, "Three Sisters Tacos" under science). The cultural and historical side of these lessons is already captured by their other tags — **Cultural Traditions**, **Native American History**, **Historical Figures**, **Colonialism's Impact**, and the lessons' cultural-heritage labels — so companion planting itself is best treated as a single gardening-science concept. We recommend keeping it as its own concept, with all 13 lessons grouped under science.
thermal_energy: Thermal Energy covers heat and heat transfer in cooking — what happens to food when it boils, bakes, fries, or melts. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**. Nearby science ideas — Mechanical Energy (tagged once), Phases of Matter (tagged twice), and States of Matter (tagged once) — are genuinely different physics concepts, so they stay separate rather than folding in here.
colonialisms_impact: Colonialism's Impact covers lessons about how colonialism shaped food, farming, culture, and the displacement of peoples — typically lessons on Indigenous foodways, the transatlantic trade, and communities uprooted in colonialism's aftermath. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**; no near-duplicates or overlaps were found. Related history concepts — Slavery, Native American History, Indigenous Knowledge, Indigenous Stories, and History of the American West (each tagged once) — cover distinct topics and stay separate.
food_webs: Food Webs covers who-eats-whom relationships in nature — producers, consumers, and decomposers — and appears in 11 lessons, usually food-chain and ecosystem-modeling activities. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**. It's related to but distinct from Ecosystems (73 lessons, the broader picture of how living systems work), Producers (tagged twice), Consumers (tagged twice), and Biodiversity (7 lessons, which is about the variety of species rather than feeding relationships).
seasonality: Seasonality covers which foods grow in which season — cooking and eating with what's local right now — and appears in 11 lessons, with two near-twins that would bring it to 24. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept** and fold two near-duplicates into it: **Seasonal Cycles** (6 lessons — despite its cycle-sounding name, almost all of its lessons are really seasonal-eating content, like Pumpkin Spice Muffins, September Salsa Toasts, and Food Miles) and **Seasonal Eating** (7 lessons squarely on the same food-availability idea, like Eating in Season and fall-harvest cooking). Its companion, Seasonal Changes, looks at the other side of seasons — watching how nature itself changes through the year — and stays a separate concept. Because seasonal-eating lessons often also touch nutrition and where food comes from, Health and Social Studies make sense as secondary subjects. One caution: the existing tags mix the two season lenses a lot, so some lessons may currently be filed under the wrong one.
sensory_exploration: Sensory Exploration covers using the five senses to explore food and the garden — taste-test bars, sensory scavenger hunts, herb smelling — mostly in lessons for the younger grades. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**. Narrower neighbors — Taste (tagged once), Sound (tagged once), and Observation (5 lessons) — focus on a single sense or on observation as a science skill, so they stay separate.
seasonal_changes: Seasonal Changes covers noticing how nature shifts through the year — signs of spring, trees changing, the cycle of the seasons — and appears in 10 lessons, every one squarely on that theme. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept** and fold in two rarely used twins: **Seasonal Change** (tagged twice — both are garden-observation lessons, Spring and Fall Special Spot) and **Seasons** (tagged twice — Summer Garden and Garden Celebration, a general review of the seasons cycle), bringing it to 14 lessons. Its companion, Seasonality, is the food side of the story — which foods grow when — and stays a separate concept, absorbing Seasonal Cycles and Seasonal Eating. Of the whole season-related family, this one is the cleanest: all of its sampled lessons fit the theme, while the others are more mixed.
drawing: Drawing covers lessons where students draw — garden journaling, plant illustration, observational sketching like leaf rubbings, and "draw what you see" exercises in the early grades. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**. An earlier look at Visual Arts (76 lessons) deliberately kept Drawing separate rather than rolling it in, because the lessons treat drawing as a distinct technique worth its own tag. Smaller one-off arts tags like Coloring and Craft Activity (each tagged once) fold into Visual Arts instead, and Painting (tagged once) is another one-off neighbor. If the line between Drawing and Visual Arts feels muddy in practice, one option is to fold Drawing into Visual Arts as a specific technique — that's the team's call.
fractions: Fractions covers fraction math in cooking and recipes — measuring ingredients, scaling recipes up or down, and visual fractions like pizza slices or halving fruit. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**. It's distinct from the other math concepts — Measurement (66 lessons), Counting (20), Data Collection (19), and Estimation (15) — and from Recipe Scaling (3 lessons), which uses fractions but is really about resizing a whole recipe.
plant_growth: Plant Growth covers how plants grow — sprouting seeds, growth stages, tracking seedling progress — mostly in early-grade plant observation lessons. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**. It's distinct from Plant Parts (239 lessons, plant anatomy), Plant Needs (41, what plants require to live), Life Cycles (43, the broader cycle idea that includes animals too), and Seeds (13). Two barely used neighbors, Growing Cycles and Growth Patterns (tagged once each), are probably too vague to stand alone and could fold into Plant Growth when the team reviews the rarely used concepts. One open thread: this concept overlaps with one of the lesson themes, and that overlap will be sorted out in a separate pass.
cultural_narratives: Cultural Narratives covers reading, writing, and telling stories rooted in a culture — the Three Sisters legend, a biography read-aloud about Georgia Gilmore, a Palestinian olive-tree story paired with making hummus and pita — and appears in 8 lessons. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**. It sits close to Cultural Traditions (206 lessons), but that one is a broad social-studies tag, while almost every Cultural Narratives lesson (7 of 8) also carries Storytelling and centers on reading or telling a culturally rooted story — a genuinely literacy-focused lens worth keeping separate. It's also distinct from Narrative Writing (3 lessons), which is about students composing their own stories rather than reading or hearing them.
photosynthesis: Photosynthesis covers how plants make their own food from sunlight — the role of chlorophyll, leaves as food factories, plant energy. The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**. Rare neighbors Cellular Respiration and Chemical Reactions (tagged once each) are genuinely different processes and stay separate, as does Plant Needs (41 lessons), which includes sunlight but covers everything a plant needs to live.
writing:
Writing is the general-purpose tag used when a lesson involves writing but no particular genre was named — it appears in 8 lessons, with about 28 more spread across the specific writing tags (roughly 36 writing lessons in all). The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**, as the home for writing lessons that don't fit a specific genre — but this depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts as a group. The genre tags are How-to Writing (5 lessons), Persuasive Writing (4), Narrative Writing (3), Opinion Writing (3), Descriptive Writing (3), Argumentative Writing (2), Informational Writing (2), Recipe Writing (2), and single uses of Comparative Writing, Creative Writing, Letter Writing, and Writing Claims.

There are three ways to go: (a) keep Writing as the catch-all alongside the genre tags — the recommendation; (b) remove Writing and always require a genre — but that loses the 8 lessons where no genre was specified; or (c) fold all the genre tags into Writing — not recommended, since opinion, narrative, and persuasive writing teach genuinely different skills.
biodiversity: Biodiversity covers the variety of living things and why diverse ecosystems are stronger — food webs, single-crop versus mixed planting, plant families, adaptation — and appears in 7 sampled lessons (6 filed under Science, 1 under Social Studies). The recommendation is to **keep as its own concept**, but treat it as a Science concept only. The lone Social Studies use, on "The Story of the 3 Sisters," doesn't add a real second meaning: that lesson already has Cultural Traditions and Companion Planting carrying the cultural side and Plant Parts and Ecosystems carrying the science side. Tellingly, another lesson that explicitly teaches both social and ecological diversity got this right — Biodiversity under Science only, with Cultural Traditions and Community Systems covering the social side. This looks like part of a wider tagging quirk, also seen with Historical Figures, Companion Planting, and Nutrition Education, where the subject a concept is filed under simply mirrors the school subjects listed for the lesson rather than what the concept itself means. Biodiversity stays distinct from Food Webs (11 lessons, feeding relationships) and Ecosystems (73 lessons, the broader systems picture).
seasonal_eating: Seasonal Eating covers which foods are available to eat in which seasons, and appears in 7 lessons — mostly cooking and plant-hunt lessons about eating what's in season. We recommend folding it into **Seasonality**, which covers the same food-availability idea and appears in 11 lessons; together the related seasonal tags span 38 lessons. Of the 7 lessons sampled here, 5 are squarely about food availability (like the Sixth Grade Summer Plant Hunt and the Food Geography pizza lesson), while 2 (The Seasons: Fall and The Seasons: Winter) are really about observing seasonal changes in nature and were mis-tagged — those fit better under **Seasonal Changes**, the companion concept that covers the nature-observation side. If "Seasonal Eating" feels like a clearer name to the team than "Seasonality," the fold can go the other direction — either way, the same lessons end up grouped together.
music: Music covers music-making in lessons — songs about plants, food, and the garden, making instruments, and listening exercises. We recommend keeping it as its own concept. Two nearby tags stay separate because they're genuinely different: **Song** (tagged once) treats lyrics as a text to write and read, more a literacy activity than music-making, and **Instrument Making** (tagged once) is a narrower craft-construction activity.
observation: Observation covers noticing and recording what's happening in the garden, and appears in 6 lessons — 5 tagged as science and 1 as art. The split looks like a tagging accident rather than a real difference: the one art-tagged lesson (a spring scavenger hunt) is a direct twin of a science-tagged fall lesson in the same Special Spot series, and it landed under art only because observation got bundled with drawing as "observational drawing." All five science-tagged lessons treat observation as a science practice (Rachel Carson nature writing, sensory scavenger hunts, watching plant needs and seasonal change), and no lesson in the sample uses observation as an art-training practice. We suggest treating Observation as a science concept, and where a lesson pairs it with drawing, tagging **Drawing** for the art activity and Observation for the science practice the drawing supports. It sits near **Sensory Exploration** (appears in 11 lessons), which is the broader five-senses concept — Observation is specifically the skill of careful noticing. Since the habit of attaching observation to whichever subject already had drawing may show up in other lessons too, it's worth checking across all lessons before settling this.
reading_comprehension: Reading Comprehension covers reading-for-understanding work — answering questions about a text, summarizing, and finding main ideas. We recommend keeping it as its own concept. The very similar tag **Reading** (tagged twice) might fold in later as a generic version of the same thing — that depends on how the team decides to organize the reading concepts, and will be weighed when the rarely-used tags are reviewed. It stays distinct from **Recipe Reading** (appears in 69 lessons, specifically about reading recipes), **Storytelling** (appears in 76 lessons, the telling-stories side), and **Cultural Narratives** (tagged 8 times, about reading cultural stories).
seasonal_cycles: Seasonal Cycles is tagged on 6 lessons, and despite its name, the lessons under it are almost all about eating foods when they're in season — so we recommend folding it into **Seasonality**. In the sample reviewed, 6 of 7 lessons are seasonal-eating content (Pumpkin Spice Muffins, the Food Miles Game, September Salsa Toasts, Tomato Sauce with Summer Vegetables, and others): what's available when to cook, not the science of changing seasons. **Seasonality** (appears in 11 lessons) is the proposed home for that food-availability idea. Lessons genuinely about watching the seasons change (signs of spring, leaf collecting) belong under **Seasonal Changes** (appears in 10 lessons) and would not move here. If "Seasonal Cycles" feels like a clearer name to the team than "Seasonality," the fold can go the other direction — the same lessons end up grouped together either way.
adaptations: Adaptations covers how plants and animals develop traits to survive in their environment, and appears in 5 lessons — spanning plant adaptations (surviving the seasons, tolerating drought) and broader ideas like variation and natural selection. Three near-identical tags fold in cleanly: **Adaptation** (tagged once), **Plant Adaptation** (tagged once), and **Plant Adaptations** (tagged once), bringing the combined total to 8 lessons. We suggest keeping it as its own concept under the plural name "Adaptations" simply because that's the most-used version; the team can switch the name to the singular "Adaptation" if that reads better — either name gathers the same lessons.
animal_needs: Animal Needs covers what animals need to live — food, water, shelter, and space — usually in early-grade habitat lessons and lessons about supporting pollinators. We recommend keeping it as its own concept; it's the animal-side partner to **Plant Needs** (appears in 41 lessons). Two nearby concepts stay distinct: **Habitats** (tagged 4 times) focuses on the environment that provides those needs, and **Pollinators** (appears in 28 lessons) covers similar territory but is specifically about pollinator ecology.
how_to_writing: How-to Writing covers step-by-step instructional writing — writing recipes, garden-task directions, and other "how to" pieces. We recommend keeping it as its own concept. The nearby tag **Recipe Writing** (tagged twice) could fold in here, since writing a recipe is one specific kind of how-to writing — that call comes when the rarely-used tags are reviewed, and it also depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts overall. It stays distinct from the other writing types — **Narrative Writing** (tagged 3 times), **Opinion Writing** (tagged 3 times), **Descriptive Writing** (tagged 3 times), and **Persuasive Writing** (tagged 4 times) — and from the catch-all **Writing** (tagged 8 times), since each is a genuinely different kind of writing to teach.
plant_identification: Plant Identification covers learning to recognize and name plants — identifying herbs, telling weeds from edibles, sorting plant families, and recognizing native plants — and appears in 5 lessons. We recommend keeping it as its own concept. Two near-duplicate tags fold in cleanly: **Identifying Plants** (tagged twice, just a different wording) and **Plant ID** (tagged once, an abbreviation), bringing the combined total to 8 lessons. It stays distinct from **Plant Parts** (appears in 239 lessons, about plant anatomy) and **Plant Needs** (appears in 41 lessons, about what plants need to grow).
social_justice_issues: Social Justice Issues covers lessons with a social-justice theme — food justice, equity, the civil rights movement, Cesar Chavez and farm labor — and appears in 5 lessons. We recommend keeping it as its own concept, with the near-duplicate tag **Social Justice** (tagged twice) folding in as a shorter version of the same label, for a combined total of 7 lessons. The team may want to weigh which name works better — "Social Justice Issues" or simply "Social Justice" (the word "Issues" reads slightly less neutral); either name gathers the same lessons. Two nearby concepts stay separate: **Environmental Justice** (tagged 3 times) is specifically about environment-related justice, and **Advocacy** (tagged 3 times) is about taking action.
bilingual_education: Bilingual Education covers teaching across two languages — Spanish-English vocabulary lessons, dual-language read-alouds, and multilingual food-vocabulary work. We recommend keeping it as its own concept. It stays distinct from **Vocabulary Development** (appears in 59 lessons), which is the broader single-language vocabulary concept.
garden_exploration: Garden Exploration is a general tag for "welcome to the garden" lessons, and appears in 4 lessons — usually early-grade, first-day-of-program introductions. This one is a genuine judgment call for the team: unlike specific science concepts such as **Plant Parts** (appears in 239 lessons) or **Pollinators** (appears in 28 lessons), it doesn't name a particular idea being taught — it mostly means "this lesson takes place in or around the garden." Similar vague one-off tags (**Garden Topics**, **General Exploration**, and **Plant Science**, each tagged once) are already recommended for removal. The team should decide whether Garden Exploration earns its keep because 4 lessons genuinely share that introduction-to-the-garden framing, or whether to remove it too — since the existing garden location filter already covers "takes place in the garden."
global_connections: Global Connections covers globalization and cultural exchange across borders — typically "where does our food come from" geography lessons and lessons about food trade. We recommend keeping it as its own concept. It stays distinct from two nearby concepts: **Geography** (appears in 56 lessons), the broader place-and-maps concept, and **Trade Routes** (appears in 16 lessons), which focuses specifically on the history and economics of trade.
habitats: Habitats covers lessons about the places animals and plants live — pollinator habitats, the variety of life in different habitats, and "what does this animal need to live" lessons. It pairs naturally with **Animal Needs** (5 lessons): Habitats is about the surrounding environment, while Animal Needs is about the resources an animal requires. It is also distinct from **Ecosystems** (73 lessons), which takes the broader view of how living things relate to each other. Recommend: keep as its own concept.
movement: Movement covers lessons that get students learning through their bodies — dance, kinesthetic activities, and body-based learning (for example, learning a dance to show what seeds need to grow). The related concepts **Dance** and **Imaginary Play** (tagged once each) are narrower or clearly different angles, and stay separate. Recommend: keep as its own concept.
nutrition: Nutrition is a general science tag that appears in 4 lessons covering nutrition content. The open question is how it relates to **Nutrition Education** (107 lessons, mostly on the health side): Nutrition might be a genuinely narrower science concept — the biology of nutrition, like nutrients, energy from food, and plant vs. animal sources — or it might just be a shorter name for the same thing. If it's its own science concept, keep as its own concept; if it's only a naming duplicate, fold into Nutrition Education. This couldn't be settled without reading the lessons themselves, so it's the team's call. The related narrower concepts **Plant Nutrition** (2 lessons), **Macronutrients** (1 lesson), and **Macromolecules** (1 lesson) are clearly distinct and stay separate.
persuasive_writing: Persuasive Writing covers lessons where students write to convince a reader, often about food-system issues they care about. The nearby concept **Argumentative Writing** (2 lessons) may be a variant — some teaching traditions treat "argumentative" as the evidence-based form for older grades and "persuasive" as the broader, emotion-allowed umbrella term — and the team can decide whether to combine them when reviewing the rarely-used concepts. It is distinct from the other writing concepts and from the catch-all **Writing** (8 lessons); this also depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts overall. Recommend: keep as its own concept.
preservation:
Preservation appears in 4 lessons, mostly covering hands-on food-preservation techniques — jam-making, pickling, and herbal infusions. Three of those are science lessons that cover the same ground as the single **Food Preservation** lesson (a fermentation lesson), so on the science side these should become one concept; folding everything together would cover 5 lessons in total. Which name wins is purely a naming preference: Preservation is the more commonly used tag, but Food Preservation is the clearer label.

The fourth lesson (Three Sisters Succotash, a social studies lesson tied to Indigenous food traditions) may be using "preservation" in a different sense — keeping Indigenous foodways alive across history, rather than learning a technique — but that's a single lesson with a thin description. The team decides: either keep Preservation as a separate culture-and-history concept alongside a combined Food Preservation (and consider giving it a clearer name, like "cultural preservation"), or treat that one tag as a fluke and fold everything into Food Preservation.
advertising: Advertising covers lessons that look critically at food marketing — media-literacy lessons like "Do we Recognize these Logos?" and critiques of how the food industry advertises. The nearby concept **Food Marketing** (1 lesson) would likely fold in cleanly; the team can decide that when reviewing the rarely-used concepts. Recommend: keep as its own concept.
advocacy: Advocacy appears in 3 lessons where students take action on food and community issues — the Food System Advocates lessons and The Lorax Debate. The single **Community Activism** lesson (Guerilla Gardening for Birds, where students make seed bursts to grow bird-friendly plants in their neighborhood) is a near-synonym and fits best here rather than under **Community Systems** (15 lessons), since it follows the same take-action shape as the existing advocacy lessons; folding it in brings the total to 4 lessons. **Social Justice Issues** (5 lessons) and **Environmental Justice** (3 lessons) stay separate — those are about understanding issues, while Advocacy is about acting on them. Recommend: keep as its own concept, with Community Activism folding into it.
descriptive_writing: Descriptive Writing covers lessons where students write rich descriptions, typically food-memory pieces describing taste, smell, and texture. The nearby concept **Descriptive Language** (1 lesson) is probably the same idea in practice — the difference between using descriptive language and writing a descriptive piece may not hold up in real lessons — and the team can decide whether to combine them when reviewing the rarely-used concepts. It is distinct from the other writing concepts; this also depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts overall. Recommend: keep as its own concept.
environmental_justice: Environmental Justice covers lessons on the environmental impact of food systems, food debates, and how pollution affects communities. Right now it overlaps with **Community Systems** (15 lessons) — at least one lesson, "Environmental Impact of the Food System," shows up under both — and to a lesser degree with **Social Justice Issues** (5 lessons); a later review should sharpen the line between these three concepts, or confirm that the overlap is intentional. **Advocacy** (3 lessons) stays separate as the taking-action concept. Recommend: keep as its own concept, with the boundary question left for the team to settle.
food_presentation: Food Presentation covers lessons that treat food as an art form — plating, food-art design, and arranging meals to look beautiful. It is distinct from **Visual Arts** (76 lessons), the broad art-making umbrella, and from **Cultural Traditions** (206 lessons), which is about the cultural side of food. Recommend: keep as its own concept.
graphing: Graphing covers math lessons where students turn data into pictures — bar graphs, pictographs, and charts of cooking or garden data. It is distinct from **Data Collection** (19 lessons), which covers working with data more broadly; graphing is the specific step of visualizing it. Recommend: keep as its own concept.
legumes: Legumes is a narrow science concept covering legume plants — beans, lentils, and peas — typically in bean-cooking and lentil lessons. The nearby concept **Plant-Based Proteins** (1 lesson) overlaps a little, since legumes are the main plant source of protein, but it takes a clearly different nutrition angle and stays separate. Recommend: keep as its own concept.
microorganisms: Microorganisms covers the biology of tiny living things — yeast, bacteria, and the organisms behind fermentation — typically in fermentation, sourdough, and decomposition lessons. The rarely-used concepts **Yeast**, **Microbiome**, and **Fermentation** (1 lesson each) are narrower relatives, and the team can consider whether Microorganisms should absorb them when reviewing the rarely-used concepts; **Decomposition** (47 lessons) covers the broader breaking-down process and stays separate. Recommend: keep as its own concept.
narrative_writing: Covers lessons where students write their own stories — personal narratives, food-memory stories, and other story writing. It's different from **Cultural Narratives** (appears in 8 lessons), which is about reading or telling culturally rooted stories, often by other authors, and from **Storytelling** (appears in 76 lessons), which is about understanding and sharing stories aloud rather than students composing them. It also sits alongside the other writing concepts, so its final shape depends partly on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts as a group. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
novel_connections: Covers lessons that connect a novel the class is reading to food, garden, or cultural themes — for example, linking Bud, Not Buddy to a jam lesson. The name is a bit unclear on its own; the team may prefer something plainer like "Literature Connections" or "Novel-to-Lesson Connections." It's different from **Recipe Reading** (appears in 69 lessons), which is about reading recipes as step-by-step text, and from **Cultural Narratives** (appears in 8 lessons), which is about reading culturally rooted stories. Recommendation: keep as its own concept, possibly with a clearer name.
opinion_writing: Covers lessons where students write opinion pieces — for example, writing up taste-test opinions or "what's your favorite" reflections. It's different from **Persuasive Writing** (appears in 4 lessons), which is about convincing an audience, and from **Argumentative Writing** (tagged in 2 lessons), which is about backing up a claim with evidence. The three reflect real grade-level distinctions in writing standards (opinion writing is the K-5 form, argument the 6-12 form, and "persuasive" an older umbrella term), which may be worth noting for teachers. How this trio lands also depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts as a group, including the general **Writing** tag (appears in 8 lessons). Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
recipe_scaling: Covers the math of resizing recipes — doubling, halving, and using ratios to adjust a recipe for the number of portions you need. Nearby math concepts stay separate: **Scaling** (tagged once, the generic math idea), **Ratios** (tagged twice), and **Unit Rates** (tagged twice) — Recipe Scaling is the recipe-specific use of that math. It's also different from **Fractions** (appears in 9 lessons), which provides the underlying arithmetic but shows up outside recipes too. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
root_vegetables: A narrow topic covering root vegetables — carrots, potatoes, beets, radishes — usually in cooking lessons built around them. The nearby concept **Roots** (tagged twice) is a different idea about plant anatomy in general, and both are treated in the lessons as specific topics in their own right rather than other names for the much broader **Plant Parts** (appears in 239 lessons). Recommendation: keep as its own concept, as a food-and-plant-specific topic.
seed_dispersal: Covers how seeds travel — by water, wind, or hitching a ride on animals — and appears in 3 lessons, all of which have students build models of seed-dispersal methods. It's kept separate from the broader **Seeds** concept (appears in 13 lessons) because it looks at seeds through an ecology lens, and **Seed Starting** (tagged once) is also separate — that one is about sprouting and propagating seeds. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
argumentative_writing: Covers writing that backs up a claim with evidence, and is tagged in only 2 lessons. Some grade-level writing standards treat "argument" (supporting a claim with evidence) as different from "persuasion" (winning over an audience), so in principle it's distinct from **Persuasive Writing** (appears in 4 lessons) — but with only 2 lessons, the tags can't reliably hold up that distinction. The right call depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts: if the general **Writing** tag (appears in 8 lessons) stays as a catch-all, this could fold into **Persuasive Writing** or stand alone; if **Writing** is removed, this could stay as its own concept; if **Writing** becomes the umbrella with the specific types folded under it, this would fold into **Writing**. No recommendation yet — it waits on that group decision.
climate: Covers climate science — regional climates and the difference between climate and weather. Its close partner **Climate Change** (tagged once) raises a question for the team: fold **Climate Change** into this concept (since climate change is a sub-topic of climate science), or keep both if the climate-change framing feels meaningfully different for teaching. Recommendation: keep as its own concept, with **Climate Change** as a candidate to fold in, pending the team's decision.
consumers: Covers the food-chain role of consumers — animals and other organisms that eat producers. It pairs naturally with **Producers** (appears in 2 lessons); both describe positions in a food web. Recommendation: fold into **Food Webs** (appears in 11 lessons), which is already the established home for food-chain lessons — producers and consumers are pieces of food-web structure. Alternatively, the team could keep **Producers** and **Consumers** as a small standalone pair if it wants those roles tagged explicitly.
discussion: Covers classroom discussion practice — accountable talk, partner conversations, and whole-class discussion. The nearby concepts **Debate** (tagged once) and **Public Speaking** (tagged once) cover narrower speaking skills; the team could eventually combine all three into a single speaking-and-discussion concept, or keep them separate. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
erosion: Covers erosion and weathering — soil washing or wearing away through water and weather. The nearby concept **Soil Science** (appears in 44 lessons) covers the broader picture of how soil forms; erosion is one specific process within that. Recommendation: keep as its own concept, though the team could fold it into **Soil Science** if it feels too narrow to stand alone.
germination: Covers the moment a seed sprouts — the specific shift from dormant seed to emerging seedling. Its neighbors cover different ground: **Life Cycles** (appears in 43 lessons) is the broader cycle-of-life frame, **Plant Growth** (appears in 9 lessons) covers growth after sprouting, and **Seeds** (appears in 13 lessons) covers seed anatomy and planting. Germination is distinguishable from all three as the moment of emergence itself. Recommendation: keep as its own concept, though the team could fold it into **Plant Growth** or **Seeds** if the boundary feels artificial.
identifying_plants: Covers identifying plants and is tagged in 2 lessons — it's the same idea as **Plant Identification** (appears in 5 lessons), just worded differently. The plan for **Plant Identification** already includes folding this in, along with **Plant ID** (tagged once), which would bring **Plant Identification** to 8 lessons total. Recommendation: fold into **Plant Identification**.
informational_writing: Informational Writing covers lessons where students write factual, explanatory pieces, and appears in 2 lessons. It's genuinely different from **How-to Writing** (5 lessons, step-by-step instructions) and **Descriptive Writing** (3 lessons, sensory detail) — the focus here is presenting facts rather than explaining a process or painting a picture. One of the two lessons is the Informational Writing topics/subtopics lesson that also carries the vague science tags Garden Topics and Plant Science. The right call depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts: if **Writing** (8 lessons) stays as a broad catch-all, this could stay on its own or fold into How-to Writing; if Writing is removed, keeping this as its own concept makes sense; or the team could fold this into Writing instead.
patterns: Patterns covers pattern recognition in math lessons — number patterns, shape patterns, and patterns in garden-bed arrangements. Nearby math concepts like **Counting** (appears in 20 lessons), **Data Collection** (19 lessons), and **Estimation** (15 lessons) are different math practices that are each staying as their own concept, and this one is distinct from all of them. Recommendation: keep as its own concept.
performance: Performance covers performing-arts activities in lessons — skits, plays, and demonstrations. A few related concepts each appear in just 1 lesson: **Dance**, **Puppetry**, **Puppet Making**, and **Instrument Making**, which cover specific kinds of performance. The team may eventually want to combine all of these under one broader performing-arts concept, but for now the recommendation is to keep this as its own concept until that bigger arts question is decided.
phases_of_matter: Phases of Matter covers the physical-science idea of solids, liquids, gases, and the changes between them. **States of Matter** (appears in 1 lesson) is the exact same idea under a different name — these two should become one concept. Since Phases of Matter is the more frequently used label, the recommendation is to keep this as its own concept and fold States of Matter into it — but which of the two names wins as the displayed label depends on the team's preference.
plant_nutrition: Plant Nutrition covers how plants take in what they need — water, nutrients from the soil, and energy from photosynthesis. It sits between several neighbors: **Plant Needs** (appears in 41 lessons) covers plant requirements broadly, **Photosynthesis** (8 lessons) covers that specific energy process, and **Nutrition** (4 lessons) is about people, not plants. Recommendation: keep as its own concept — it's narrower than Plant Needs but not the same thing as Photosynthesis. The team may prefer to fold it into Plant Needs if the dividing line feels artificial.
poetry: Poetry shows up in two different subject areas, tagged once each: once as a reading-and-writing topic and once as an arts topic. Both angles are genuinely real — reading or composing poems on the literacy side, and reciting or performing poems (spoken word) on the arts side — so this isn't a tagging mistake like some other double-subject cases were. Recommendation: keep as its own concept, treated mainly as a reading-and-writing topic with the arts angle as secondary. The team should confirm whether to keep both subject homes or pick just one.
producers: Producers covers the food-chain idea of organisms — mostly plants — that make their own food through photosynthesis. It pairs naturally with **Consumers** (appears in 2 lessons) and with **Food Webs** (11 lessons), which is the established home for food-chain lessons. Recommendation: fold into **Food Webs**, since that concept already covers who-eats-whom relationships. Alternatively, the team could keep Producers and Consumers as a matched pair if they want lessons tagged explicitly at that level of detail.
protein: Protein covers protein as a nutrient in food and nutrition lessons. Several related concepts sit nearby: **Macromolecules** and **Macronutrients** (1 lesson each), **Nutrition** (4 lessons), **Nutrition Education** (107 lessons), and **Plant-Based Proteins** (1 lesson) for the plant-protein subtopic. Recommendation: keep as its own concept — though folding it into **Macronutrients** (treating protein as one example of a macronutrient) would capture the same lessons. Either choice works; it comes down to which name the team prefers to see.
ratios: Ratios covers ratio and proportional reasoning in math lessons, usually through recipes — ingredient ratios and scaling amounts up or down. **Unit Rates** (appears in 2 lessons) is closely related, since a rate is a specific kind of ratio, and **Recipe Scaling** (3 lessons) is where ratio reasoning gets applied in the kitchen. Recommendation: keep as its own concept, with Unit Rates a good candidate to fold into it later (ratios is the broader idea, unit rates a sub-application) — pending the team's decision.
reading: Reading is a general literacy tag that appears in 2 lessons without a specific focus like comprehension or a particular genre. Recommendation: fold into **Reading Comprehension** (6 lessons), which is the established concept for reading-for-understanding lessons — this tag looks like a shortened version of the same label. The team could instead keep Reading separate if those 2 lessons aren't actually comprehension-focused (for example, read-alouds or quiet independent reading). Either way, this depends on how the team decides to organize the reading concepts overall — Reading, Reading Comprehension, Narrative Reading, Biography Reading, and Informational Text all need sorting out together.
recipe_writing: Recipe Writing covers lessons where students write their own recipes — a specific kind of step-by-step writing with attention to ingredients and amounts. It has already been flagged as a natural fit under **How-to Writing** (appears in 5 lessons), since writing a recipe is really a form of how-to writing. Recommendation: fold into **How-to Writing** — but this depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts. If **Writing** (8 lessons) is kept as a broad catch-all, Recipe Writing could still fold into How-to Writing, or remain its own concept.
research: Research covers research practices in literacy lessons — gathering information, citing sources, and organizing findings. The related concept **Note-Taking** (appears in 1 lesson) is one specific research skill. Recommendation: keep as its own concept; the team may later want to combine Research and Note-Taking into one concept when lessons get re-tagged.
roots: Roots covers roots as a plant part and appears in 2 lessons. **Plant Parts** (appears in 239 lessons) is the established home for all plant-anatomy tagging, and this small tag looks like it drifted off from that bigger concept. Note that **Root Vegetables** (3 lessons) is related but genuinely different — it's about growing and eating root crops like turnips, carrots, and beets, not about plant anatomy. Recommendation: fold into **Plant Parts**.
scientific_method: Covers lessons that teach science as a process — making a hypothesis, running an experiment, and drawing conclusions. Recommended: keep as its own concept. It is closely related to **Scientific Claims** (which appears in just 1 lesson) — both are about *doing* science rather than learning science facts. The team could fold Scientific Claims into this one, or keep both side by side; that part is the team's call.
seasonal_change: Covers how the seasons change through the year, and appears in 2 lessons — it is simply the singular form of **Seasonal Changes**, which appears in 10 lessons. Recommended: fold into **Seasonal Changes**. Together with the **Seasons** tag, the combined concept would cover 14 lessons. One heads-up: a related set of "Seasons" lessons got split between this tag and **Seasonal Eating**, so some lessons may be sitting under the wrong seasonal concept — the seasonal group (**Seasonal Changes**, **Seasonality**, **Seasonal Eating**, **Seasonal Cycles**) overlaps and is worth a look as a set.
seasons: A general label for lessons about the seasons, tagged on 2 lessons — really the same idea as **Seasonal Changes** (10 lessons). Recommended: fold into **Seasonal Changes**. The 2 lessons here belong to a related set of "Seasons" lessons that ended up split across different seasonal concepts, so checking that they land in the right place is worth doing alongside the other seasonal tags (**Seasonality**, **Seasonal Eating**, **Seasonal Cycles**).
sequencing: Covers putting things in order — events in a story, steps in a recipe, or steps in a procedure. Recommended: keep as its own concept. It is genuinely different from **Reading Comprehension** (6 lessons), which is about understanding a text as a whole, and from **Vocabulary Development** (59 lessons), which is about learning individual words — so there is no real overlap problem here.
social_justice: Covers lessons on fairness and justice in the food system, tagged 2 times — it is a shorter name for the same idea as **Social Justice Issues** (5 lessons). Recommended: fold into **Social Justice Issues**, for a combined 7 lessons. If the team prefers the shorter name, it could just as easily go the other way — keep "Social Justice" as the name and fold Social Justice Issues into it. Either way the same 7 lessons end up together; it is purely a question of which name reads better.
spices: Covers lessons about spices — taste and flavor, the chemistry of cooking with them, and the spices used in different cuisines around the world — and appears in 2 lessons. Recommended: keep as its own concept. One thing to weigh: it currently sits with the science topics, but the lessons lean more cultural and culinary than scientific, so the team could also treat it as a food-culture topic instead — or remove it if 2 lessons feels too narrow to be worth a tag.
tool_use: Covers using scientific tools — microscopes, scales, measuring instruments — in garden and cooking lessons, and appears in 2 lessons. Recommended: keep as its own concept. The nearby **Simple Machines** (1 lesson) covers physics-style tools like levers and pulleys; Tool Use is the broader practice of measuring and observing with instruments. The team may prefer to remove it if the 2 lessons are not really *about* tool use as a skill being taught.
unit_rates: Covers unit-rate math — figuring price per pound, miles per hour, and similar "per one" calculations. Recommended: keep as its own concept. A unit rate is a specific kind of ratio, so this overlaps with **Ratios** (2 lessons): keeping it gives explicit unit-rate tagging, while folding it into **Ratios** keeps things simpler. Either way the math content stays findable — it is a question of how fine-grained the team wants these tags to be.
volume: Covers measuring volume — capacity, cubic units, and the volume of ingredients in recipes. Recommended: fold into **Measurement** (66 lessons), which is the established home for measurement lessons — though keeping Volume as its own more specific concept is also reasonable. This depends on how the team decides to organize the measurement concepts: **Volume**, **Weight** (1 lesson), **Area** (1 lesson), and **Perimeter** (1 lesson) could all fold into Measurement, or stay as separate, more specific tags alongside it.
adaptation: Covers how living things adapt to their environment, tagged 1 time — it is simply the singular form of **Adaptations** (5 lessons). Recommended: fold into **Adaptations**; once this and the related plant-adaptation tags fold in, the combined concept covers 8 lessons. If the team prefers the singular name ("Adaptation" reads more like a concept; "Adaptations" more like a list), it could fold the other direction instead — either way the same 8 lessons end up together, so it is purely a naming preference.
animal_welfare: Covers the ethics of how animals are treated in the food system — factory farming, humane treatment, vegetarianism. Recommended: keep as its own concept. The closely related **Factory Farming** (1 lesson) covers much of the same ground; together the two concepts cover food ethics. Down the road, the team could consider combining both under a single broader food-ethics concept.
area: Covers calculating area — the area of a garden bed or a baking pan. Recommended: fold into **Measurement** (66 lessons), the established home for measurement lessons — though the team could keep it if explicit area tagging is wanted. This depends on how the team decides to organize the measurement concepts: **Area**, **Perimeter**, **Volume**, and **Weight** could all fold into Measurement, or stay as separate, more specific tags.
argumentation: Covers making and defending a claim out loud — the spoken side of building an argument. Recommended: keep as its own concept, though it could also fold into **Debate** (1 lesson) or **Discussion** (2 lessons). Its written counterpart is **Argumentative Writing** (2 lessons), and **Public Speaking** (1 lesson) is also nearby — so the right answer here depends on how the team decides to organize the writing and discussion concepts as a group.
beneficial_insect_identification: This tag is for lessons on spotting helpful insects in the garden — pollinators and the bugs that eat pests — and it is rarely used. It overlaps heavily with **Pollinators** (appears in 28 lessons), which already covers pollinator ecology and likely covers the helpful-insect angle too, and it touches on **Plant Identification** (appears in 5 lessons) on the identification-skill side. The suggestion is to fold into **Pollinators**; removing it is the other option if it feels too narrow to keep.
biography: This tag marks biography as a reading genre and is tagged in just 1 lesson. It is nearly identical to **Biography Reading** (also tagged once). The suggestion is to fold the two into one concept — or fold both into **Storytelling** (appears in 76 lessons), which is already the home for stories and for biographies of food-history figures like Cesar Chavez, Rachel Carson, and Dr. Carver. The right answer depends on how the team decides to organize the reading concepts.
biography_reading: This tag is for lessons where students read biographies, and it is tagged in just 1 lesson. It is essentially the same idea as **Biography** (also tagged once). The suggestion is to fold the two into one concept, or fold both into a broader home: **Storytelling** (appears in 76 lessons), **Reading Comprehension** (appears in 6 lessons), or **Cultural Narratives** (appears in 8 lessons) — the last is arguably where biographies of food-history figures belong, read as cultural stories. This depends on how the team decides to organize the reading concepts.
biotic_abiotic_factors: This tag covers the difference between the living and non-living parts of an ecosystem, and it is rarely used. It sits inside the much broader **Ecosystems** (appears in 73 lessons) — the living/non-living distinction is really vocabulary used within ecosystem lessons rather than a separate topic. The suggestion is to fold into **Ecosystems**; keep as its own concept only if the team feels this specific distinction deserves its own tag.
cardiovascular_system: This tag covers the heart and blood system in human-body lessons and is tagged in just 1 lesson. It names the same body system as **Circulatory System** (also tagged once) — "cardiovascular" is the more medical term, while "circulatory" is the word most K-12 classrooms use. The suggestion is to fold into **Circulatory System** so there is one concept for this system; which name to keep is the team's call.
categorization: This tag covers grouping and classifying items by their features, and it is rarely used. It pairs closely with **Sorting** (tagged once) — both describe the same classification practice. The suggestion is to combine the two into one concept, likely keeping the Categorization name as the more formal teaching term — so keep this one and fold **Sorting** into it — or keep both as their own concepts if the team wants a separate, kindergarten-friendly Sorting tag.
cellular_respiration: This tag covers how living cells turn food into energy, and it appears in just 1 lesson. It is the partner concept to **Photosynthesis** (appears in 8 lessons) — together the two make up the energy cycle taught in biology. The case for keeping it as its own concept is to preserve that pairing in the tag list; the case for removing it is that one lesson may be too little to justify a tag. Keep wins if the team wants matching coverage on both sides of the photosynthesis pair.
chemical_reactions: This tag covers chemical reactions, especially the kitchen kind — cooking chemistry and fermentation as a chemical change — and it is rarely used. The nearby **Fermentation** tag (tagged once) is a specific cooking application of this broader idea. The suggestion is to keep as its own concept under the name "Chemical Reactions"; the team could later combine the related chemistry topics (this one plus **Fermentation** and **Yeast**) when lessons get re-tagged.
circulatory_system: This tag covers the heart and blood system in human-body lessons and is tagged in just 1 lesson. It names the same body system as **Cardiovascular System** (also tagged once) — "circulatory" is the word most K-12 classrooms use, while "cardiovascular" is the more medical term. The suggestion is to combine the two into one concept — likely keeping this one and folding **Cardiovascular System** into it — though the final name is the team's call.
civil_rights_movement: This tag covers the civil rights movement as a historical topic, and it is rarely used. It likely shows up alongside **Historical Figures** (appears in 68 lessons) on lessons about people like Georgia Gilmore and Cesar Chavez, while **Social Justice Issues** (appears in 5 lessons) covers broader social-justice themes. The suggestion is to keep as its own concept — it is specific enough as a historical movement to stand on its own, distinct from the broader social-justice category.
climate_change: This tag covers climate change specifically, and it is rarely used. It is one topic within the broader **Climate** tag (appears in 2 lessons). The suggestion is to fold into **Climate**, since climate change is a specific phenomenon within climate science — or keep both as their own concepts if the team wants a distinct Climate Change tag for environmental-justice and advocacy lessons.
coloring: This tag marks coloring as an art activity and is tagged in just 1 lesson. It is a small technique within **Visual Arts** (appears in 76 lessons), the broad home for art activities, which already has a standing proposal to absorb both this tag and **Craft Activity** (also tagged once). The suggestion is to fold into **Visual Arts**, as already proposed.
colors_of_plants: This tag marks lessons where students looked at the colors of plants, and it is rarely used. It is more a loose lesson topic ("today we looked at plant colors") than a distinct concept. The options: fold into **Plant Parts** (appears in 239 lessons), since plant anatomy includes coloration; fold into **Sensory Exploration** (appears in 11 lessons), treating color observation as a sensory practice; or remove as too narrow. The lean is to fold into **Plant Parts** — the team decides.
community: Community is tagged in 1 lesson and covers much the same ground as Community Systems, which is tagged in 15. The shorter name looks like a stray variation of the same label rather than a separate idea. Community Systems currently stretches across three different ideas — food systems, environmental systems, and how communities are organized — and this one tag most likely fits the how-communities-are-organized side. Recommendation: fold into Community Systems.
community_activism: Community Activism is tagged in 1 lesson ("Guerilla Gardening for Birds") and covers taking action to change things in one's community. Despite the similar name, it doesn't really belong with Community Systems (tagged in 15 lessons), which is about how communities and systems are structured — activism is about taking action, which pairs naturally with Advocacy (tagged in 3 lessons). Recommendation: fold into Advocacy.
community_building: Community Building is tagged in 1 lesson and is essentially another wording of Community Systems, which is tagged in 15. If kept separate, it would sit on the how-communities-are-organized side of Community Systems' territory — that concept currently also stretches to cover food systems and environmental systems, and the team may eventually tighten its boundaries. Recommendation: fold into Community Systems; if the team later narrows what Community Systems means, this tag could also simply be removed.
comparative_writing: Comparative Writing covers lessons where students write side-by-side comparisons of two things. It is genuinely different from the other writing types in the collection — Descriptive Writing (3 lessons), Narrative Writing (3), Persuasive Writing (4), and Opinion Writing (3) — because the emphasis is on analyzing similarities and differences. Whether to keep it as its own concept or fold it into a broader writing concept depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts.
craft_activity: Craft Activity is tagged in 1 lesson and describes a kind of activity (the lesson involves crafting) rather than a topic students learn about. Recommendation: fold into Visual Arts (tagged in 76 lessons). Alternatively, the team may judge it too vague to keep at all — it works more like a note about what happens in the lesson than a real concept — in which case remove.
creative_writing: Creative Writing covers imaginative writing practice in lessons, but it is currently filed under Arts rather than Language Arts — almost certainly a tagging mistake, since creative writing is normally a language-arts skill. What to do with it depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts: if the broad Writing concept (tagged in 8 lessons) stays as the catch-all, fold into Writing; if Writing is removed in favor of the specific writing types, keep this as its own concept, refiled under Language Arts.
dance: Dance covers dance and movement-based arts in lessons. It overlaps with Movement (tagged in 4 lessons), which is the broader idea — dance is one specific kind of movement. Recommendation: fold into Movement, unless the team wants lessons to be explicitly findable under dance, in which case keep as its own concept.
debate: Debate covers formal debate practice in lessons — students arguing a chosen or assigned position. It sits near several related speaking-and-arguing concepts: Argumentation (tagged in 1 lesson), Discussion (2 lessons), and Public Speaking (1 lesson). Recommendation: keep as its own concept under the name "Debate"; alternatively, the team could combine this whole group into one broader discussion-and-speaking concept.
descriptive_language: Descriptive Language covers using sensory words, adjectives, and vivid imagery in writing and speaking. It overlaps with Vocabulary Development (tagged in 59 lessons), the broader word-skills concept, and with a small group of related tags — Figurative Language (1 lesson), Similes (1), and Sensory Details (1). Recommendation: fold into Vocabulary Development; alternatively, the team could combine that small group into a single Figurative Language concept — this depends on how the team decides to organize the figurative-language concepts.
design: Design is a vague tag in lessons — it could mean visual design, graphic design, garden design, or the design-thinking process, and it isn't clear which. Recommendation: keep as its own concept, but only with a clearer name (for example "Visual Design" or "Garden Design") so future tagging stays consistent; otherwise remove as too vague.
engineering: Engineering covers engineering topics in lessons, but it is currently filed under Arts, which looks like a tagging mistake — engineering conventionally belongs with Science. The nearby Science concepts Simple Machines (tagged in 1 lesson) and Mechanical Energy (1 lesson) cover related building-and-physics ideas. Recommendation: move it to Science and fold into Simple Machines, or keep as its own concept under Science — the team's call on the name and subject placement.
environmental_impact: Environmental Impact appears in 1 lesson and covers the effects people have on the environment, in a fairly general way. It sits close to Environmental Justice (tagged in 3 lessons), which has a speaking-up-for-change angle, and to Community Systems (15 lessons), which includes an environmental-systems side. Recommendation: fold into Environmental Justice; alternatively, keep as its own concept if the team wants a neutral environmental-effects tag that is separate from the justice and advocacy framing.
environmental_stewardship: Environmental Stewardship covers caring responsibly for the environment — a take-care-of-it framing, which is different from the speaking-up-for-change framing of Environmental Justice (tagged in 3 lessons). Environmental Impact (1 lesson) is also nearby. Recommendation: fold into Environmental Justice — possibly renaming that concept to something broader that welcomes both framings — or keep as its own concept under the name "Environmental Stewardship". The team picks.
factory_farming: Factory Farming covers industrial-scale food and animal production and its problems, and appears in just 1 lesson. Recommend keep as its own concept. Several other rarely used tags sit nearby — **Animal Welfare**, **Farm Labor**, **Food Marketing**, and **Food Systems** (each tagged once) — so another option is to combine these food-ethics topics under one broader new concept if the team prefers. Also worth noting: **Community Systems** (15 lessons) may already cover the food-systems side of this topic.
family_traditions: Family Traditions covers foods and customs passed down within a family, and is tagged in just 1 lesson. Recommend fold into **Cultural Traditions** (206 lessons), which is already slated to absorb this tag along with **International Food Traditions**. Family traditions are really cultural traditions where the cultural group is a family rather than an ethnic or national community, so the broader concept covers it well.
farm_labor: Farm Labor covers the history and experience of farm workers — lessons about Cesar Chavez and migrant labor — and appears in just 1 lesson. Recommend keep as its own concept. Related rarely used tags include **Factory Farming**, **Slavery**, and **Civil Rights Movement** (each tagged once), so the team could instead combine these under a broader labor-history concept if that feels more useful.
feedback_systems: Feedback Systems covers feedback loops in biology and ecosystems — how bodies and habitats keep themselves in balance — and is a rarely used tag. Recommend keep as its own concept. The nearby tag **Homeostasis** (tagged once) covers the body-regulation side specifically, so the team could instead combine the two under a broader biological-systems concept. It would also be reasonable to remove this one as too advanced for most K-12 lessons.
fermentation: Fermentation covers how yeast and bacteria transform food — bread rising, pickling, food-preservation chemistry — and appears in 1 lesson. Recommend keep as its own concept. It connects closely to **Preservation**: the Sandor Katz "Food Hero" lesson, currently tagged **Food Preservation**, is really about fermentation. Nearby tags **Yeast** and **Chemical Reactions** (each tagged once) cover related kitchen chemistry, so another option is to combine all of these under one broader cooking-chemistry concept.
figurative_language: Figurative Language covers metaphor, simile, personification, and imagery in writing, and is a rarely used tag. Recommend keep as its own concept and let it absorb **Similes** (tagged once), and possibly **Descriptive Language** and **Sensory Details** (each tagged once) as well. Another option is to fold all of these into **Vocabulary Development** (59 lessons) as the broader word-craft concept. This depends on how the team decides to organize the figurative-language concepts.
food_marketing: Food Marketing covers how food is advertised and packaged — especially advertising aimed at children — and appears in 1 lesson. Recommend fold into **Advertising** (3 lessons), since food marketing is advertising applied specifically to food. Keeping it as its own concept only makes sense if the team wants food marketing tagged separately from general advertising.
food_memory: Food Memory covers personal memories tied to food — likely from a personal-narrative or food-memoir reading or writing lesson — and is a rarely used tag. Recommend fold into **Cultural Narratives** (8 lessons), which covers culturally rooted storytelling and often involves exactly this kind of food-memory framing. Removing it as too narrow would also be reasonable; this one is the team's call.
food_preservation: Food Preservation covers ways of keeping food from spoiling — canning, pickling, fermenting — and appears in 1 lesson. Recommend fold into **Preservation** (4 lessons), bringing that concept to 5 lessons; this combination was already proposed, and the science side is straightforward since the one lesson here (the Sandor Katz "Food Hero" lesson) is about fermentation, which is food-preservation chemistry. One separate open question remains for **Preservation** itself: one of its lessons (Three Sisters Succotash) uses "preservation" in the cultural sense rather than the food sense, and the team should decide how to handle that.
food_processing: Food Processing is a vague tag — it could mean industrial food manufacturing or everyday kitchen prep — and is rarely used; its science tagging suggests the industrial, food-chemistry meaning. Recommend keep as its own concept, though it's a soft call. Nearby tags **Preservation** (4 lessons), **Fermentation**, and **Chemical Reactions** (each tagged once) cover related food chemistry, so folding everything into one broader food-chemistry concept is an option — as is removing this tag as too vague.
food_systems: Food Systems covers how food moves from farm to table across a community or economy, and appears in 1 lesson as a concept tag. There is no firm recommendation yet, because "Food Systems" also exists as one of the library's broader themes, and **Community Systems** (15 lessons) already serves as the food-systems lens within the concepts. The most likely outcome is to remove the concept-side tag, let the Food Systems theme carry that idea, and narrow **Community Systems** to community structures only — but this depends on how the team decides to organize the themes alongside the concepts.
