garden_topics: Garden Topics is a vague science tag that appears on 1 lesson — an English-language-arts lesson about organizing informational writing into topics and subtopics. Recommend remove. That lesson only uses gardening and cooking as brainstorming material; it doesn't actually teach any plant or garden science, so the tag was filler rather than a real concept (the lesson's other science tag, **Plant Science**, is recommended for removal for the same reason).
general_exploration: General Exploration appears on just 1 lesson, and that lesson is an unfinished blank template that was never filled in. Recommend remove. The tag is leftover placeholder scaffolding rather than a real concept being taught, and the lesson itself is flagged separately for cleanup (either delete it or recover its content).
growing_cycles: Growing Cycles is a loosely defined tag about how plants grow and change over time, and it appears in just 1 lesson. The same ground is already covered by two existing concepts: **Life Cycles** (43 lessons), which takes the broader view of cycles in both plants and animals, and **Plant Growth** (9 lessons), which focuses specifically on the stages of plant growth. We recommend fold into **Plant Growth** because it's the more precise match, though **Life Cycles** is the more established home — either could work, and it makes sense to decide this together with the very similar tag Growth Patterns.
growth_patterns: Growth Patterns is a vague tag about patterns in how plants grow, and it's one of the rarely used tags. Everything it covers is already handled by **Plant Growth** (9 lessons), so we recommend fold into **Plant Growth**; removing it as too fuzzy to be useful is also reasonable. It's in the same boat as **Growing Cycles** (tagged once), another vague science tag pointing at the same fold options — deciding both the same way will keep things tidy.
harvest: Harvest covers picking ripe crops in the garden, and it's tagged in just 1 lesson. It has a twin, **Harvesting** (also 1 lesson) — the same idea under a slightly different word. We recommend folding the two into a single concept; which name survives ("Harvest" or "Harvesting") is the team's preference. Once combined, the team could also consider folding the result into a broader plant concept such as **Life Cycles** or **Plant Growth** if a separate harvest tag doesn't feel necessary.
harvesting: Harvesting covers the activity of picking crops in the garden, and it's tagged in just 1 lesson. It's a twin of **Harvest** (also 1 lesson) — the same idea, just a different word form. We recommend folding the two into one concept; the team can pick whichever name reads better. Depending on the team's preference, the combined concept could then also fold into a broader plant concept such as **Life Cycles** or **Plant Growth**.
historical_context: Historical Context marks lessons that use a historical time period as the setting or backdrop, and it appears in just 1 lesson. It sits very close to **Historical Figures** (68 lessons), which is about specific people in history. The question is whether "a lesson set in a historical era" is genuinely different from "a lesson about historical people." If the team sees them as the same idea, fold into **Historical Figures** (which would then cover 69 lessons); if the backdrop angle feels like its own way of looking at history, keep as its own concept. With only one lesson using it, the usage alone can't settle this — it really needs the team's subject-matter judgment.
history_of_the_american_west: History of the American West covers US westward-expansion history, and it likely comes from a single lesson on that topic. Our lean is keep as its own concept — it's specific and clear about what it means. But the team could reasonably decide it's too narrow for the shared list and remove it; or, if the lesson is really about people and places rather than the historical period itself, its content could be covered by **Historical Figures** (68 lessons) and **Geography** (56 lessons) instead. The real question is how specific the team wants concept tags to get.
holidays: Holidays is a general tag for holiday-themed lessons; it's one of the rarely used tags and has a near-twin in **National and Religious Holidays** (tagged once). Holiday lessons — Diwali, Thanksgiving, Día de los Muertos, Eid — are really lessons about cultural traditions, and there's already a strong, well-used home for them: **Cultural Traditions** (206 lessons). We recommend fold into **Cultural Traditions** for both holiday tags, since they cover the same content.
homeostasis: Homeostasis covers how living things keep their internal conditions stable — an advanced biology topic that shows up only rarely. The closely related **Feedback Systems** (tagged once) describes the mechanism behind it. Our lean is keep as its own concept for upper-grade science lessons, but the team could instead remove it as too advanced or narrow for this collection, or combine it with **Feedback Systems** under one broader concept about how living systems regulate themselves. This is the team's call.
hydration: Hydration covers drinking enough water as a healthy habit; it's one of the less-common tags and one of only three health-focused concepts in the list, alongside **Nutrition Education** (107 lessons) and **Healthy Choices** (30 lessons). It's a specific, practical health practice rather than part of the broader nutrition picture. We recommend keep as its own concept — folding it into **Healthy Choices** is possible, but with only three health concepts total, collapsing further would thin out health coverage.
imaginary_play: Imaginary Play covers pretend and role-play activities for the youngest students, and it's one of the rarely used tags. It's distinct from the nearby performing-arts concepts — **Movement** (4 lessons), **Music** (6 lessons), **Dance** (tagged once), and **Performance** (2 lessons). We recommend keep as its own concept so early-grade arts activities stay findable, though removing it as too narrow is defensible. A bigger option worth weighing: combining the whole pretend-and-performance family — Imaginary Play, **Performance**, **Puppetry**, and **Dance** — under one broader performing-arts concept.
indigenous_knowledge: Indigenous Knowledge covers Indigenous perspectives and traditional knowledge — Three Sisters planting, traditional ecological knowledge, Indigenous foodways — and it's tagged in just 1 lesson. It travels with two equally rare neighbors, **Indigenous Stories** (1 lesson) and **Native American History** (1 lesson), so Indigenous content is currently scattered across three tiny tags. (Lessons also carry a separate Cultural Heritage label that covers Indigenous identity, so concept tags aren't the only place this content is marked.) The options: combine all three into a single Indigenous-perspectives concept, fold them into **Cultural Traditions** (206 lessons), or keep each as its own concept. This depends on how the team decides to organize the Indigenous-related concepts — best decided for all three together.
indigenous_stories: Indigenous Stories covers Indigenous narratives told in story form, and it's tagged in just 1 lesson — currently filed under social studies, even though story-based content usually sits with the reading and writing concepts. It overlaps with **Cultural Narratives** (8 lessons) and **Storytelling** (76 lessons). The options: fold into **Cultural Narratives** (treating it as story content), combine it with **Indigenous Knowledge** (1 lesson) and **Native American History** (1 lesson) into a single Indigenous-perspectives concept, or fold into **Cultural Traditions** (206 lessons). This depends on how the team decides to organize the Indigenous-related concepts, so it's best decided alongside those.
informational_text: Informational Text covers reading nonfiction — making sense of factual, encyclopedia-style texts — and it's one of the rarely used tags; it's the reading-side mirror of **Informational Writing** (2 lessons). We recommend fold into **Reading Comprehension** (6 lessons), where understanding informational texts fits naturally as one kind of reading work; keeping it as its own nonfiction-reading concept is the alternative. This depends on how the team decides to organize the reading concepts, so it's best settled together with the rest of that group.
instrument_making: Instrument Making covers lessons where students build their own musical instruments, and it is one of the rarely used tags. We recommend folding it into **Music** (tagged in 6 lessons), treating instrument-building as one way music shows up in lessons. Other small making-and-crafting tags sit nearby — Puppet Making and Craft Activity, each tagged once. The alternative is to keep it as its own concept if the team wants hands-on building activities tracked separately from listening to or performing music — your call.
international_food_traditions: International Food Traditions covers lessons about food customs from around the world, and it is tagged just once. It is a narrower take on **Cultural Traditions** (tagged in 206 lessons), with the emphasis on cuisines tied to particular countries and regions, and Cultural Traditions was already proposed to absorb it (along with Family Traditions). We recommend folding it into **Cultural Traditions**.
letter_writing: Letter Writing covers lessons where students write letters — personal, formal, or letters that advocate for something — and it is rarely tagged. It is one of the writing-genre concepts, so the right answer depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts overall. If the team keeps the general **Writing** concept (tagged in 8 lessons) as a catch-all, Letter Writing would likely fold into **Writing**; if the team drops the general Writing tag in favor of specific genres, Letter Writing should stay. Until that decision, the lean is to keep as its own concept.
literary_elements: Literary Elements covers lessons that explore the building blocks of stories — character, setting, plot, and theme — and it is rarely tagged. Nearby small tags — Figurative Language, Descriptive Language, Similes, and Sensory Details, each tagged once — cover the related craft of figurative language, and what happens to those depends on how the team decides to organize the figurative-language concepts. We recommend keeping this one as its own concept, with the figurative-language tags handled separately; the alternative is to combine all of them under one broader literary-craft concept if the team prefers.
macromolecules: Macromolecules covers the chemistry of what food is made of — proteins, carbohydrates, and fats — and it is tagged in just one lesson. It sits very close to **Macronutrients** (also tagged once) and **Protein** (tagged twice); together these cover the science of nutrients. The options are to keep it as its own concept, fold into **Macronutrients** as the broader food-chemistry idea, or remove it as too advanced for K-12 lessons. This depends on how the team decides to organize this small group of nutrition-science concepts.
macronutrients: Macronutrients covers the three big nutrient groups — protein, carbohydrates, and fat — and it is tagged in just one lesson. We recommend keeping it as its own concept and letting it absorb the closely related **Protein** (tagged twice) and **Macromolecules** (tagged once), though it could also stand alongside Protein as a separate, narrower tag. The wider set of nutrition-related concepts — this one plus Protein, Macromolecules, Plant-Based Proteins, Plant Nutrition, Nutrition, and Nutrition Education — is sprawling, so the final shape depends on how the team decides to organize that group.
map_reading: Map Reading covers lessons where students practice working with maps — reading legends, finding coordinates, and identifying regions — and it is rarely tagged. We recommend folding it into **Geography** (tagged in 56 lessons), the established home for geography lessons, treating map reading as one of its skills. The alternative is to keep it as its own concept if the team wants lessons that specifically teach map skills to be findable apart from broader geography content.
mechanical_energy: Mechanical Energy covers the physics of motion and stored energy, and it is rarely tagged. Related tags include Simple Machines (tagged once) and **Thermal Energy** (tagged in 12 lessons), which is the established energy concept. We recommend keeping it as its own concept; the alternative is to combine Mechanical Energy and Simple Machines under one broader physics concept if the team prefers.
microbiome: Microbiome covers lessons about communities of microbes — like the microbes living in our gut or in garden soil — and it is rarely tagged. It is a specific slice of **Microorganisms** (tagged in 3 lessons), the broader microbe concept, so we recommend folding it into **Microorganisms**.
narrative_reading: Narrative Reading covers lessons where students read stories, and it is rarely tagged. It belongs to a group of overlapping reading tags — Reading, Reading Comprehension, Narrative Reading, Biography Reading, and Informational Text — so the answer partly depends on how the team decides to organize the reading concepts. We recommend folding it into **Reading Comprehension** (tagged in 6 lessons), since reading a story is comprehension applied to stories; the alternative is to keep it as its own concept if the team wants story-reading tagged on its own.
national_and_religious_holidays: National and Religious Holidays covers lessons built around holiday celebrations, and it is rarely tagged. It pairs with the nearly identical Holidays tag (tagged once); in our lessons, holidays usually show up as moments of cultural tradition, and the name here is unusually wordy for a topic tag. We recommend folding both this and **Holidays** into **Cultural Traditions** (tagged in 206 lessons), which absorbs this content cleanly.
native_american_history: Native American History covers lessons about Native American history, and it is rarely tagged. It is one of three closely related tags — alongside **Indigenous Knowledge** and **Indigenous Stories** — so the right answer depends on how the team decides to handle the Indigenous-related concepts as a group. (The library's cultural-heritage tags already include Native American as a heritage category.) The options are to combine the three into one shared Indigenous-perspectives concept; fold into **Historical Figures** (tagged in 68 lessons) and **Geography** (tagged in 56 lessons) where lessons center specific people and places; fold into **Cultural Traditions** (tagged in 206 lessons); or keep as its own concept, which is the lean for now pending that group decision.
note_taking: Note-Taking covers lessons where students practice taking notes, usually as part of research or reading for information, and it is rarely tagged. It pairs closely with **Research** (tagged twice) and **Organizing Ideas** (tagged once). We recommend folding it into **Research**, treating note-taking as one of its skills; the alternative is to combine Research, Note-Taking, and Organizing Ideas under one broader research-skills concept — your call.
organizing_ideas: This concept covers planning and organizing ideas before writing or researching, and it appears only rarely in lessons. It sits close to **Note-Taking** (tagged in 1 lesson) and **Research** (tagged in 2 lessons), which cover similar writing- and research-process skills, and it's also related to the broader group of writing concepts. The suggestion is to fold into **Research**, but this partly depends on how the team decides to organize the writing concepts.
painting: Painting is a specific art technique that appears in just 1 lesson. It follows the same pattern as **Coloring** and **Craft Activity** (each tagged in 1 lesson), which are already proposed to fold into **Visual Arts** — the broad arts concept that appears in 76 lessons. The suggestion is to fold into **Visual Arts** as another art technique under that umbrella.
perimeter: Perimeter covers measuring the distance around a shape, and it appears only rarely in lessons. It sits at the edge of the measurement group alongside **Volume** (tagged in 2 lessons), **Area** and **Weight** (1 lesson each), and **Measurement** itself, which appears in 66 lessons and is the established home for measurement lessons. The suggestion is to fold into **Measurement**, though it could also keep as its own concept — this depends on how the team decides to organize the measurement concepts.
pests: Pests covers garden pests — identifying them and managing them — and appears only rarely in lessons. Its natural partner is **Beneficial Insect Identification** (tagged in 1 lesson), which covers the helpful-versus-harmful insects idea from the other side, and **Pollinators** (appears in 28 lessons) is nearby too. The suggestion is to keep as its own concept, though the team could instead group the insect concepts together — Pollinators is widely used and could absorb the beneficial-insects side, with Pests staying as its complement.
photojournalism: Photojournalism — telling a story through photographs — appears in just 1 lesson. It's more of a media or arts topic than a reading-and-writing one, and a single lesson is too little to judge by. The suggestion is to keep as its own concept only if the team confirms the lesson genuinely centers on photojournalism as a storytelling genre; otherwise remove it, or treat it as an arts concept instead.
plant_adaptation: Plant Adaptation covers how plants adjust to their environment, and it appears in 1 lesson. It's a narrower, plant-only version of **Adaptations** (appears in 5 lessons), which is already proposed to absorb it along with **Adaptation** and **Plant Adaptations** (1 lesson each). The suggestion is to fold into **Adaptations**, following that proposal.
plant_adaptations: Plant Adaptations is simply the plural form of **Plant Adaptation**, and it appears in 1 lesson. Like its singular twin, it's already proposed to fold into the broader **Adaptations** concept (appears in 5 lessons), along with **Adaptation** (1 lesson). The suggestion is to fold into **Adaptations**, following that proposal.
plant_and_animal_similarities: This concept covers early-grade science comparisons between plants and animals, and it appears in 1 lesson. The closest established home is **Life Cycles** (appears in 43 lessons), which already frames growth and change for both plants and animals. With only one lesson behind it, the signal is thin — the suggestion is to fold into **Life Cycles**, though removing it is also reasonable if the team finds it too vague or too tied to one lesson's framing.
plant_based_proteins: This concept covers protein from plant sources — beans, legumes, and nuts — and appears only rarely in lessons. Related concepts include **Protein** (tagged in 2 lessons), **Macronutrients** and **Macromolecules** (1 lesson each), **Legumes** (appears in 3 lessons), and the much broader **Nutrition Education** (appears in 107 lessons). The suggestion is to fold into **Legumes**, the specific home for plant-protein lessons, though folding into **Protein** or **Nutrition Education** would also work — this is the team's call.
plant_id: Plant ID is just a shortened name for **Plant Identification**, and it appears in 1 lesson. It's already on the list of name variants proposed to fold into **Plant Identification** (appears in 5 lessons), alongside **Identifying Plants** (tagged in 2 lessons). The suggestion is to fold into **Plant Identification**, following that proposal.
plant_reproduction: Plant Reproduction covers how plants make new plants — flowers becoming seeds, pollination, and fruit as a seed carrier — and appears only rarely in lessons. It's closely tied to **Pollinators** (appears in 28 lessons), **Seeds** (13 lessons), **Life Cycles** (43 lessons), and **Plant Growth** (9 lessons). The suggestion is to keep as its own concept, since it's distinct from Pollinators (which focuses on the bees and butterflies themselves rather than the reproductive process) — though folding into **Life Cycles** is the alternative. This is the team's call.
plant_science: Plant Science is a vague catch-all tag that appears on 1 lesson — a writing lesson about topics and subtopics that just used cooking and gardening as brainstorming material. The lesson is really teaching a literacy skill, not plant science; the science tags on it (this one and **Garden Topics**, also suggested for removal) were filler rather than real concepts. The suggestion is to remove.
planting: Planting covers the hands-on activity of putting seeds or plants in the ground, and it appears only rarely as a tag — it reads more like a description of what the class did that day than a distinct concept. Related concepts include **Seeds** (appears in 13 lessons, covering seed anatomy and planting), **Plant Growth** (9 lessons), and the single-lesson tags **Seed Starting**, **Spacing**, **Harvest**, and **Harvesting**. The suggestion is to fold into **Seeds**, which naturally absorbs planting-as-activity, though removing it as too vague is also an option — this is the team's call.
problem_solving: Problem Solving covers lessons where students work through a challenge step by step, and it is one of the rarely used tags in the library. It currently sits with the reading-and-writing topics, which looks like a tagging mistake — problem solving usually belongs with math, or spans many subjects at once. There is no obvious other concept to fold it into. The recommendation is to keep it as its own concept (likely re-homed alongside the math concepts), though removing it as too vague is also reasonable — this one is the team's call.
public_speaking: Public Speaking covers lessons where students present or speak in front of others, and it is rarely tagged. It sits alongside the other speaking-related concepts: Debate (tagged once), Argumentation (tagged once), and Discussion (tagged twice). The recommendation is to keep it as its own concept — presenting to a group is different enough from back-and-forth conversation to stand alone. Alternatively, the team could combine all the speaking-related concepts into one broader speaking-and-listening concept; that's a team decision.
puppet_making: Puppet Making covers the craft of building puppets, paired with Puppetry (tagged once), which covers performing with them. The recommendation is to fold it into **Visual Arts** (appears in 76 lessons), the established home for craft activities, with Puppetry going to the performing-arts side (Performance, Dance). Other options: keep Puppet Making and Puppetry together as a small pair, or remove both as too narrow. The team should decide which way to go.
puppetry: Puppetry covers performing with puppets, and is paired with Puppet Making (tagged once), which covers building them. The recommendation is to keep it as its own concept, with Puppet Making as its partner. Other options: combine it with Movement (4 lessons), Music (6 lessons), Performance (2 lessons), and Dance (1 lesson) under one broader performing-arts concept, or remove it as too narrow — the team's call.
read_aloud: Read-Aloud marks lessons where the teacher reads a book aloud to students — a teaching technique rather than a topic the lesson is about, and a rarely used tag. Most read-aloud lessons are already covered by **Storytelling** (appears in 76 lessons), the established home for reading and telling stories. The recommendation is to fold it into Storytelling; removing it is the other reasonable option, since a technique isn't really a concept.
recipe_selection: Recipe Selection is a vague, rarely used tag that could mean either choosing among recipes or working with recipes as reading material. Either way, **Recipe Reading** (appears in 69 lessons), the established broad recipe concept, likely already covers this content. The recommendation is to fold it into Recipe Reading.
scaling: Scaling covers the math of adjusting recipe quantities — doubling, halving, or tripling ingredients — and is just a shortened version of the Recipe Scaling label. The recommendation is to fold it into **Recipe Scaling** (appears in 3 lessons); the two names describe the same thing.
scientific_claims: Scientific Claims covers lessons where students make a claim and back it up with evidence — a core science practice, and a rarely used tag. It pairs closely with **Scientific Method** (tagged in 2 lessons), the broader doing-science concept. The recommendation is to fold it into Scientific Method as one part of that practice; keeping it as its own concept is also defensible if the team wants claim-and-evidence work to stay visible on its own.
seed_starting: Seed Starting covers starting seeds indoors or in trays, and is a rarely used tag. **Seeds** (appears in 13 lessons) is the established broad concept covering seed anatomy and planting, and its own card already notes that seed starting belongs to the same family. The recommendation is to fold it into Seeds as one activity within that topic.
sensory_details: Sensory Details covers using taste, smell, and touch details in student writing, and is tagged just once. It belongs to the small family of figurative-language concepts — Figurative Language (tagged once), Descriptive Language (tagged once), and Similes (tagged once) — with **Vocabulary Development** (59 lessons) as the bigger word-craft concept nearby. The recommendation is to fold it into Figurative Language; folding it into Vocabulary Development instead is the other option. The right choice depends on how the team decides to organize the figurative-language concepts as a group.
similes: Similes covers the specific writing device of comparing one thing to another using "like" or "as," and is tagged just once. It is naturally a sub-topic of Figurative Language (also tagged once) and sits in the same family as Descriptive Language and Sensory Details, with **Vocabulary Development** (59 lessons) as the bigger word-craft concept nearby. The recommendation is to fold it into Figurative Language; folding it into Vocabulary Development instead is the other option. This depends on how the team decides to organize the figurative-language concepts as a group.
simple_machines: Simple Machines covers the six classic machines — lever, pulley, wedge, screw, inclined plane, and wheel-and-axle — and is a rarely used tag. It pairs closely with Mechanical Energy (tagged once), the other physics tag in the library. The recommendation is to keep it as its own concept; alternatively, the team could combine it with Mechanical Energy under one broader physics concept — the team's call.
slavery: Slavery covers slavery as historical content — likely tied to a lesson on US slavery, the African diaspora, or food-and-labor history — and appears in just 1 lesson. Related history concepts include Historical Figures (68 lessons), Colonialism's Impact (12 lessons), Civil Rights Movement (tagged once), and Farm Labor (tagged once). The recommendation is to keep it as its own concept: it is distinct from the broader Colonialism's Impact and from the people-centered Historical Figures, and even with only one lesson the topic carries enough weight to warrant its own tag.
soil: **Soil** is tagged on just 1 lesson and covers the same ground as **Soil Science**, our main home for soil lessons. That one lesson most likely just got the shorter label by accident. We recommend folding it into **Soil Science**, which appears in 44 lessons and is the established home for soil-related lessons.
song: **Song** is a rarely used tag for lessons built around song lyrics, currently filed under literacy rather than the arts. That placement may simply reflect a lesson that treats lyrics as a text to read rather than music to perform. **Music**, which appears in 6 lessons, is the established home for music content. The options: fold into **Music** (and treat it as an arts topic), keep as its own concept if the team wants "song as a kind of text" in the literacy lineup, or remove it as too narrow. The lean is to fold into **Music**, but this is the team's call.
sorting: **Sorting** is a rarely used tag for classifying activities — sorting plants by leaf shape or foods by color — usually in early-grade lessons as a stepping stone to math.
